alsoKnownAs: Ahli’s Hap (incorrect — refers to the separate species Sciaenochromis ahli)

nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shorelines and intermediate sandy zones of Lake Malawi; frequents rock mounds and boulder piles along the lake’s littoral zone

decorPreferences: large boulder and rock pile structures with caves and crevices
hardy plants such as Anubias or Java Fern if desired (may be uprooted)
minimal wood — does not reflect natural Lake Malawi biotope

diet.notes: Piscivore in the wild — preys on fry and juveniles of other cichlids (Copadichromis, Protomelas) by chasing them out of schools. In captivity readily accepts high-quality large sinking pellets, frozen krill, brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, and chopped seafood. Live or frozen whole feeder fish can be offered occasionally but are not required. Avoid plant-based foods and mammalian meat (beef heart). Overfeeding is a common cause of bloat in Malawi haps.

goodWith: non-mbuna haplochromines (Nimbochromis venustus, Cyrtocara moorii, Protomelas spp., Copadichromis spp.)
larger Plecos tolerant of alkaline water
avoidWith: mbuna (mouthbrooding rock-dwelling cichlids) — incompatible temperament and habitat overlap
any fish small enough to be swallowed (predation risk)
similarly coloured Haplochromine males (intense aggression and territory disputes)
other male Sciaenochromis fryeri (severe inter-male aggression)

summary: A stunning Lake Malawi haplochromine cichlid; males display an intense iridescent blue body with orange-red fin accents. An active, mid-water predatory hap that eats juvenile fish in the wild. Intermediate care due to size, aggression management, and hard-alkaline water requirements. Often confused with the closely related Sciaenochromis ahli, which is a distinct species.
careNotes:
Sciaenochromis fryeri is a large haplochromine cichlid endemic to Lake Malawi and one of the most visually spectacular freshwater fish available in the hobby. Adult males reach up to 20 cm and display an extraordinary metallic blue coloration over the entire body, often accented with orange or red on the fins; females and juveniles are comparatively drab and grey-brown, making sexing of young fish challenging. This striking juvenile-to-adult colour transition means unsexed juveniles sold in the trade may look nothing like the finished male.

As a non-mbuna hap, S. fryeri occupies rocky shoreline and intermediate sandy zones rather than the surface algae-grazing niche of mbunas. It is a predator that in the wild pursues fry and juveniles of other cichlids, so any tankmates small enough to be eaten will be eaten. It should not be mixed with mbuna; the mbuna’s hardier temperament and herbivorous digestive system leads to mismatched aggression and dietary conflicts.

A tank of at least 300 litres with a minimum 150 cm footprint is needed for a harem group (one male, 3–5 females). A single specimen can be housed in 200 L but a harem produces more natural behaviour and distributes male aggression. Rockwork should be substantial — large boulders and cave structures are essential for female refuge during spawning. Sandy substrate is preferred. Maintain Lake Malawi water chemistry: pH 7.6–8.8, gH 10–25°H, temperature 24–28°C. Regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly) are critical; high nitrates trigger bloat, a common killer of Malawi cichlids.

This species is often mislabelled and confused with Sciaenochromis ahli (“Ahli’s Hap”) in the trade — S. ahli is a distinct species described from fewer specimens and is far less commonly kept. Authentic S. fryeri stock should be sourced from reputable importers with documented provenance.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; breeding is moderately straightforward when a harem of one male with three or more females (ideally five or more) is maintained. Males select territories near sloping rocks or cave entrances and display intensified colouration during courtship. The female deposits 30–100 eggs on a flat rock surface; the male fertilises them using egg-spot markings on his anal fin (T-position spawning). The female immediately takes the eggs into her buccal cavity and broods them for approximately 21–25 days at 26°C. She does not eat during brooding; a separate holding or breeding tank is recommended to protect brooding females and maximise fry yield. Free-swimming fry are relatively large at release and can accept newly hatched brine shrimp and finely crushed cichlid flakes from day one. Remove the female to a recovery tank with food once fry are released. Subsequent broods are typically produced every 4–6 weeks under stable conditions.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Sciaenochromis fryeri
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (up to 20 cm SL), temperature 24–28°C, pH 7.6–8.8, hardness 10–25°H, minimum tank 300 L (150 cm × 45 cm × 45 cm), diet (predatory, live/frozen/dried), compatibility notes, breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, harem setup, ~3-week brooding), native range (Lake Malawi rocky littoral), sexual dimorphism notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Sciaenochromis fryeri
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Lake Malawi including Taiwan Reef, Chinyankwazi and Chinyamwezi Islands), depth range (10–40 m), diet classification (piscivore), IUCN status (Least Concern), described by Konings 1993. FishBase SL max 11.5 cm (standard length for males) vs SeriouslyFish 20 cm TL — discrepancy is standard-length vs total-length measurement convention; 20 cm TL used per SeriouslyFish and TFH primary sources.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — It’s Electric: Sciaenochromis fryeri in the Home Aquarium
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (males 17–20 cm, females 12–15 cm), temperature 24–28°C, pH 7.8–8.6, minimum tank 284 L (75 US gal) for single specimen, harem breeding (minimum 4 females, ideally 8+), female oral incubation ~3 weeks, 30–70 eggs per clutch, compatibility with Synodontis and similarly-sized haps.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Sciaenochromis fryeri Iceberg
additional.notes: Referenced for species identification notes, confusion with S. ahli in the trade, and piscivorous diet (preys on fry of Copadichromis, Protomelas). Site returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch; data retrieved via search index excerpt.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Electric Blue Cichlid (Sciaenochromis fryeri)
additional.notes: Cross-checked lifespan (7 years average, up to 10 years), pH upper bound (7.6–8.8), diet (carnivore, accepts pellets and frozen foods), juvenile colour phase notes, and compatibility (avoid bumblebee cichlids, fin-nippers, and small fish).

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; harem of 1 male to 3–5+ females recommended. Female broods 30–100 eggs orally for ~21–25 days. Separate holding tank improves fry yield. Not difficult under good conditions but requires adequate space and group composition.
